coredata.dc:title: Highly dispersed Co9S8 loaded on Fe3O4 modified by graphene quantum dots for ascorbic acid detection

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel core–shell nanomaterial, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, was synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. The material consists of Fe3O4 modified by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N‑GQDs) as the core, with highly dispersed Co9S8 forming the outer shell layer. The N-GQDs, by regulating the particle size of N-GQDs/Fe3O4, introduce more oxygen-containing functional groups to N-GQDs/Fe3O4, providing anchoring sites for Co ions and facilitating the growth of Co9S8. In the presence of H2O2, the colorless 3,3́,5,5́-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was easily oxidized to blue oxTMB by N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, indicating the significant peroxidase activity of the material. This activity is due to the combined interaction with hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2) identified by electron spin resonance (EPR) and free radical scavenging experiments. When ascorbic acid (AA) binds to oxTMB, oxTMB reacts with AA to regenerate colorless TMB. Based on the catalytic ability of the developed nanocomposite, a monitoring system for ascorbic acid was developed, with a detection limit as low as 0.404 µM and a good linear response between 1 and 70 µM. Additionally, we successfully applied this system to real sample analysis, achieving recoveries ranging from 96.01 % to 103.97 %.

head.ce:title: Highly dispersed Co9S8 loaded on Fe3O4 modified by graphene quantum dots for ascorbic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis
College of Geography and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we first synthesized N‑GQDs using a hydrothermal method and incorporated the synthesized N‑GQDs into the synthesis process of Fe3O4, resulting in the formation of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 composites. Subsequently, the core–shell material N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 with magnetic properties were synthesized through a two-step procedure involving hydrothermal synthesis and calcination, using N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 as the core and oxygen-containing functional groups as anchoring sites for Co ions. As a result, the synthesized nanomaterials exhibit strong peroxidase activity and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. Based on this, a colorimetric detection system for peroxidase activity, utilizing N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, was proposed for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) and successfully applied in both experimental and practical settings.

In this study, a novel core–shell nanomaterial, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, was synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. The material consists of Fe3O4 modified by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N‑GQDs) as the core, with highly dispersed Co9S8 forming the outer shell layer. The N-GQDs, by regulating the particle size of N-GQDs/Fe3O4, introduce more oxygen-containing functional groups to N-GQDs/Fe3O4, providing anchoring sites for Co ions and facilitating the growth of Co9S8. In the presence of H2O2, the colorless 3,3́,5,5́-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was easily oxidized to blue oxTMB by N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, indicating the significant peroxidase activity of the material. This activity is due to the combined interaction with hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2) identified by electron spin resonance (EPR) and free radical scavenging experiments. When ascorbic acid (AA) binds to oxTMB, oxTMB reacts with AA to regenerate colorless TMB. Based on the catalytic ability of the developed nanocomposite, a monitoring system for ascorbic acid was developed, with a detection limit as low as 0.404 µM and a good linear response between 1 and 70 µM. Additionally, we successfully applied this system to real sample analysis, achieving recoveries ranging from 96.01 % to 103.97 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), as a water-soluble vitamin widely found in fresh vegetables and fruits consumed daily [1,2], plays a crucial role as an antioxidant in the biochemical processes of both humans and animals[3,4]. AA is essential for normal bodily functions, and its deficiency is linked to various diseases, including cardiovascular, neurological, and cancer-related conditions[5–7]. However, humans lack the necessary enzymes to synthesize AA and must obtain it from external sources, such as food [8]. Therefore, developing rapid and accurate methods to detect AA with high selectivity and sensitivity is crucial in pharmaceuticals, the food industry, and disease diagnosis. In recent years, a variety of detection methods have been established to quantify AA, including high-performance liquid chromatography[9,10], voltammetry[11–13], chemiluminescence[14], and colorimetric analysis[15–18]. Among these detection methods, the colorimetric assay has attracted extensive attention due to its low cost, simplicity, and convenience, making it a preferred choice for everyday applications.

The term “nano enzymes” was first introduced by Flavio Manea and his colleagues[19]. In 2007, Gao et al[20]. reported for the first time that Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibited peroxidase activity, revealing their intrinsic biological effects and the novel properties of nanomaterials. Since then, growing interest in nano enzymes has led to the design and synthesis of numerous functional nano enzymes with enzyme-like properties. These nano enzymes have been widely applied across various fields, including biomedicine, environmental protection, and the food industry[21–28]. Since its discovery, Fe3O4, one of the first nanomaterials with enzymatic activity, has attracted significant interest. While the catalytic activity of Fe3O4 is relatively limited when used alone, its unique magnetic properties offer notable advantages in green chemistry. In 2022, Wang et al.[29] synthesized CAT-Fe3O4@ZIF-8 nanomaterials with magnetic properties for phenol colorimetric detection. In addition to exhibiting strong peroxidase-like activity, the materials' excellent magnetic properties enabled easy recovery and reuse. Notably, CAT-Fe3O4@ZIF-8 retained 80 % of its original activity after at least five consecutive cycles.

Transition metal sulfides (TMSs), composed of transition metals and sulfur, exhibit structures that depend on the coordination mode of the metal atoms, which are primarily octahedral or trigonal[30]. They typically have a layered structure, with sulfur atoms positioned between layers of transition metal atoms, imparting unique physical, chemical, and optoelectronic properties to these materials[31–34]. The inherent mixed valence and metallic nature of Co9S8, an important transition metal sulfide, make it highly promising for applications in electrochemical energy storage [35–38], electrocatalysis [39–41], and gas electrodes[42]. However, transition metal sulfides often exhibit limited catalytic activity when used alone due to their large particle sizes and the confinement of active sites primarily to the edges, while most basal surfaces remain inert[30]. Co9S8 is no exception, as it tends to form large particles with poor nanoscale distribution, which restricts the exposure of active sites and consequently results in low catalytic performance. Consequently, the catalytic activity of Co9S8 remains relatively weak when used solely as a catalyst[43]. In their work, Zhou et al.[30] proposed two primary strategies to enhance the catalytic performance of transition metal sulfides: (1) accelerating electron transfer within the catalyst and (2) increasing the number of accessible active sites. Therefore, introducing specific materials to regulate the particle size of Co9S8 and expose its internal active sites is a key approach to improving its catalytic activity.

Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) are widely studied and applied due to their intrinsic electronic and optical properties, chemical versatility, ease of manipulation, and biocompatibility[30,44–46]. Co9S8, a commonly used material in electrochemical catalysis and energy storage, benefits significantly from the use of carbon materials as conductive substrates, which enhance its catalytic activity and promote the dispersion of active sites. Xie et al.[47] successfully synthesized Co9S8/graphene hybrids via a hydrothermal method, where Co9S8 alone preferentially underwent lamellar assembly due to the nanoscale Kirkendall effect. The presence of graphene introduced numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, which served as anchoring sites for Co2+. This facilitated a specific crystal growth mechanism, while the site-blocking effect slowed the convergence of Co9S8 on the graphene surface, leading to the formation of smaller-sized Co9S8 particles. The synthesized Co9S8/graphene electrode delivered an impressive charge capacity of 540 C/g within 1 min and retained 74.5 % of its capacitance over a discharge time of 14 s. In the context of colorimetry, Li et al.[43] synthesized Co9S8/C nanomaterials with dual enzyme activity by incorporating carbon dots. The surface-terminal groups of the carbon dots acted as stabilizers for Co9S8, thereby controlling the size of the nanomaterials and minimizing aggregation. Gao et al.[48] introduced a two-dimensional porphyrin with a large conjugated π-system and synthesized a two-dimensional porphyrin-Co9S8 composite with peroxidase activity by exploiting the coordination between the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of H2TCPP and the Co atoms in Co9S8.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of zero-dimensional carbon materials, possess π-conjugated disk-like nanostructures and ultra-small particle sizes, with a framework similar to that of graphene (GR). Therefore, GQDs typically exhibit properties similar to graphene, such as edge and size effects, photoluminescence, and anisotropy[49–52]. Moreover, due to their ultra-small particle size, the surface of GQDs provides a large number of functional groups, which can serve as active sites for highly reactive reactions[53,54]. However, doping heteroatoms such as N, B, S, and P into GQDs replaces carbon atoms in the sp2 and sp3 hybridization networks with N, B, S, or P, thereby broadening the π-electron resonance and enhancing the optical and electrical properties of GQDs[52]. Since nitrogen is closest to carbon in the periodic table, N doping can increase the electron density by injecting electrons into the GQDs, forming surface states, and capturing excited-state electrons[51,52]. Additionally, N doping in the six-membered ring structure of graphite promotes the formation of functional groups such as pyridine, pyrrole, and graphitic nitrogen, enhancing the surface chemical activity and modulating the electronic structure of the material[30].

Therefore, as shown in Scheme 1 , we first synthesized N‑GQDs using a hydrothermal method and incorporated the synthesized N‑GQDs into the synthesis process of Fe3O4, resulting in the formation of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 composites. The presence of N‑GQDs not only regulates the morphology of Fe3O4 but also helps achieve the desired particle size, as confirmed by characterization. More importantly, doping with N‑GQDs introduced additional oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4. Subsequently, the core–shell material N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 with magnetic properties were synthesized through a two-step procedure involving hydrothermal synthesis and calcination, using N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 as the core and oxygen-containing functional groups as anchoring sites for Co2+. The synthesized N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 nanocomposites are small in size and exhibit excellent peroxidase-like activity. In the presence of H2O2, the material efficiently catalyzes the oxidation of TMB, producing the blue product oxTMB, which turns the solution from colorless to blue. When AA is introduced, it undergoes a reductive reaction with oxTMB, leading to a fading of the blue color. Based on this phenomenon, we have developed a colorimetric detection system that leverages the peroxidase-like activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 for highly sensitive detection of ascorbic acid.

The citric acid (C6H8O7), urea (CH4N2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), thiourea (CH4N2S), anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa), trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O), cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), dextrose (C6H12O6), NaCl, KCl, p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2), tert-butanol (C4H10O2), sucrose (C12H22O11) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent. Ammonia (NH3·H2O), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), acetic acid (CH3COOH), glycine (C2H5NO2), L-arginine (C6H14N4O2), L-histidine (C6H9N3O2), L-tryptophan (C11H12N2O2), ethanol (C2H5OH), aspartic acid (C4H7NO4), terephthalic acid (C8H6O4), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. All chemicals used were analytically pure and did not require further purification. The water used throughout the experiments was ultrapure water.

The morphological and structural properties of the catalyst were monitored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEITECNAI20, USA). The elemental composition of the materials was analyzed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The material composition was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2400, Japan) under the following test conditions: scanning rate of 5°/min. The elemental composition, atomic valence, and content of the materials were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The magnetic properties of the samples were measured with a vibrating sample magnetometer (EV9). The carbon in the material was verified using Raman spectroscopy (excitation wavelength of 532 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV–vis absorption spectrometer (UV–vis, MAPADA P4).

Following published methods, N-GQDs were synthesized[55]. In particular, 2.1 g of citric acid (C6H8O7) and 1.8 g of urea (CH4N2O) were weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was continuously stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 3 h to ensure complete dissolution and homogenization. The solution was then transferred to a sealed autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3,500. The dialysis water was replaced every 12 h to ensure effective purification. Subsequently, the dialyzed solution was concentrated to 5 mL and stored for further use.

To prepare the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 composites, 0.675 g of ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and 0.3 g of trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) were accurately weighed and added to a beaker. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of N‑GQDs and 40 mL of ethylene glycol were introduced into the mixture, which was stirred for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. Next, 1.2 g of sodium acetate (CH3COONa) was added, and the mixture was stirred for another 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to an autoclave reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. Different volumes of N‑GQDs (0.75 mL, 1.5 mL, and 3 mL) were used to optimize the N–GQDs to Fe3O4 ratio. These samples were referred to as 0.75 N‑GQDs/Fe3O4, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4, and 3 N‑GQDs/Fe3O4, respectively. TEM was used to examine the morphology and structural characteristics of the synthesized composites.

0.036 g of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4, 0.145 g of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), 0.046 g of thiourea (CH4N2S), and 0.18 g of glucose (C6H12O6) were weighed and added to 40 mL of ethylene glycol. The mixture was ultrasonicated until fully dissolved. Subsequently, 3 mL of ammonia solution was added drop by drop and sonicated for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 190 °C for 12 h. After cooling, it was washed three times with deionized water and alcohol, and baked in an oven at 60°C for 6 h, and the product was designated as N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy. The N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy composite was placed in a tube for calcination under nitrogen (N2) from room temperature to 600 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The calcination was maintained at 600 °C for 2 h. The calcined product of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 was obtained after naturally cooling down to room temperature.

The peroxidase activity of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 was investigated using H2O2 and TMB as substrates. A mixture containing 1850 µL of acetic acid-sodium acetate (HAc-NaAc) buffer (pH 3.5), 50 µL of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 suspension (1 mg/mL), 50 µL of H2O2 (0.1 M), and 50 µL of TMB (5 mM) was prepared. The mixture was incubated for 20 min at room temperature, and the absorption spectra of the system were recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy. The peroxidase activity of Fe3O4, N‑GQDs, Co9S8, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4, and N‑GQDs/Co9S8 was investigated under the same experimental conditions.

To optimize the peroxidase activity of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, analysis of the different reaction conditions of the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 volume (10 ∼ 100 µL, 1 mg/mL), TMB (20 ∼ 200 µM), H2O2 (0.5 ∼ 4 mM)， pH (3 ∼ 5.5) and temperature (20 ∼ 40 ℃) on the enzyme activity of peroxidase.

The POD-like activity of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 was analyzed by steady-state kinetics by changing the concentrations of H2O2 or TMB, respectively. The experimental procedure was conducted as follows: 50 µL of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 suspension (1 mg/mL), 50 µL of TMB (1.6 ∼ 12 mM), and 50 µL of H2O2 (20 ∼ 180 mM) were mixed in a 5 mL centrifuge tube. The final solution volume was adjusted to 2 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.5). After thorough mixing, the absorbance of the reaction mixture at 652 nm was monitored at 30-second intervals to track the reaction progress and determine stabilization kinetics.

The experimental data were analyzed using the Lineweaver-Burk plot to determine the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction rate (Vmax), based on the Michaelis-Menten equation:

Where V0 represents the initial rate, Vmax represents the maximum reaction rate, Km represents the Mie constant, and

Represents the substrate concentration. The lower the value of Km, the stronger the affinity of the enzyme for the substrate.

Experiments were conducted under optimal conditions to assess the system's response to AA. Briefly, 50 µL of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 suspension (1 mg/mL), 50 µL of H2O2 (0.1 M), and 50 µL of TMB (5 mM) were added to 1.8 mL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.5). After 20 min of incubation, 50 µL of AA at varying concentrations (0.2 ∼ 2.2 mM) was added to the system. Subsequently, the absorbance of the reacted system at 652 nm was analyzed by UV–vis.

The feasibility of AA detection by the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 enzyme was evaluated by using purchased vitamin C tablets and Water-soluble C100 drink. The purchased vitamin C tablets were crushed into powder and dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. After 30 min of sonication, the solution was centrifuged to extract the supernatant, which was then saved for further use. After filtering the Water-soluble C100 drink through a 0.22 μm membrane to get rid of any insoluble particles, it was put aside. After that, the Water-soluble C100 drink was diluted to a detectable concentration using ultrapure water. The devised technique was used to measure the amount of AA in the diluted product. The sample was then mixed with known amounts of AA solution, and the mixture's absorbance was measured.

The crystal structures of the catalysts were analyzed by XRD. a and b in Fig. 1 A show the synthesized N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 and Co9S8, respectively, which correspond to the standard cards JCPDS: 88‑0315 and JCPDS: 19‑0364, respectively. Due to the low content of N-GQDs, the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 does not exhibit any diffraction peaks. In Fig. 1A, pattern c shows the uncalcined N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy. It can be seen that the diffraction peaks at 35.52°, 43.17°, and 62.70°corresponded to the (311), (400), and (440) planes of the Fe3O4 lattice (JCPDS: 88‑0315), while the diffraction peak at 29.92° corresponds to the (311) planes of the cubic Co9S8 lattice (JCPDS 75‑1610). However, pattern d shows the final material, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 after calcination. The diffraction peaks appearing at 31.38°, 47.86°, and 52.03° correspond to the (222), (511), and (440) crystallographic planes of the cubic Co9S8 lattice (JCPDS: 19‑0364). The diffraction peaks at 30.06°, 35.48°, 43.1°, and 62.84° correspond to the (220), (311), (400), and (440) crystal planes of the Fe3O4 lattice (JCPDS: 88‑0315). The XRD pattern did not exhibit any significant diffraction peaks related to N-GQDs, likely due to their low content.

To gain a more objective understanding of the material's composition, the defects in the sample were analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). For the N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 sample, Raman peaks at 664.2 cm−1, corresponding to the Co9S8 bands [43,56], and those at 1351.8 and 1576.7 cm−1, attributed to the D and G carbon bands of N-GQDs, were clearly observed[57]. The intensity ratio of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) was 1.39, indicating that the material has a high defect density. These results confirm the successful synthesis of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8.

Fig. 2 A shows the TEM image of N-GQDs, which shows that N-GQDs are uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 2.2 nm. Comparing the TEMs of Fe3O4 (Fig. 2B) and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 2C), N-GQDs/Fe3O4 with the addition of N-GQDs is more dispersed and has a smaller particle size than Fe3O4. This is because the hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs provide anchor localization points that can control the growth of nanoparticles, control their growth rate, and prevent particle aggregation. In order to select the optimal ratio of quantum dots, Fe3O4, 0.75 N-GQDs/Fe3O4, N-GQDs/Fe3O4, and 3 N-GQDs/Fe3O4 were characterized using TEM. The average particle sizes of Fe3O4, 0.75 N-GQDs/Fe3O4, and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 were 120.2 nm (Fig. S1 A), 94.9 nm (Fig. S1 B), and 86.9 nm (Fig. S1 C), respectively. However, 3 N-GQDs/Fe3O4 was irregular in shape and did not generate more uniform spherical particles (Fig. S1 D). As a result, we finally decided to use N-GQDs/Fe3O4 as the core of the core–shell composite after adding 1.5 mL quantum dots.

Fig. 2D and F display the TEM images of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy and N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, while Fig. 2H and I show the corresponding EDX spectra. According to the EDX data, the calcined material's Co, S, and Fe element content considerably increased. Combined with the XRD data pattern of the material (Fig. 1A), it can be hypothesized that only a small amount of Co9S8 was generated before calcination. However, after calcination, a large amount of Co9S8 was formed. This was verified by high-magnification transmission electron micrographs of the material.

Fig. 2E presents the high-magnification diffractogram of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy, where it is evident that the primary component of the material is Fe3O4, with a lattice spacing of 0.483 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystallographic plane of Fe3O4. Additionally, a small amount of Co9S8 with a lattice spacing of 0.298 nm is observed at the periphery of Fe3O4, consistent with the (311) crystallographic plane of Co9S8. Fig. 2G shows the high magnification diffractogram of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, where the lattice spacing of the outer material is 0.575 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystallographic plane of Co9S8. This crystalline facet is present in large quantities in the material, indicating a substantial increase in the synthesis of Co9S8. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of the inner core is approximately 0.295 nm, which aligns with the (220) crystal plane of Fe3O4, further confirming the successful synthesis of the core–shell structured N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 nanomaterials. The synthesized N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 nanomaterials are spherical, with a particle size of approximately 130 nm, and exhibit a uniform dispersion. In contrast, the morphology of Co9S8 alone, observed using TEM (Fig. S2), reveals an irregular shape and significant inhomogeneity in material distribution. Therefore, the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 nanomaterials, with N‑GQDs/Fe3O4 as the core, exhibit smaller particle sizes and better dispersibility compared to pure Co9S8.

XPS was employed to confirm the material composition both before and after calcination, with the results displayed in Fig. S3. The full XPS spectrum revealed characteristic peaks for S 2p, C 1 s, N 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p, and Co 2p. The upper panel of Fig. 3 corresponds to N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy, while the lower panel represents the final synthesized nanomaterial, N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8.

The peaks of C 1s in Fig. 3A, the peak at 284.8 eV result from the C=C bond, and a peak at 286.3 eV corresponds to C‑O functional groups. For N 1s spectra shown in Fig. 3B, the peaks corresponding to pyridine N and graphite N of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy are located at 399.6 eV and 402.2 eV, respectively. In the case of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, pyridine N and graphite N appear at 398.9 eV and 402.2 eV, respectively. Beyond, the additional fitted peak at 400.8 eV results from pyrrole N. The presence of pyrrole N indicates the introduction of a new active site[58–60]. For O 1s spectra (Fig. 3C), the peaks of N‑GQD/Fe3O4@CoxSy at 533.0 eV, 531.7 eV, and 530.5 eV are attributed to C‑O bonds, metal oxides, and metal carbonates, respectively. These are exhibited in the O 1s spectra of N‑GQD/Fe3O4@Co9S8 at 533.8 eV, 532.2 eV, and 530.3 eV.

Additionally, for Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 3D), the peaks at 710.8 eV (Fe 2p1/2) and 724.0 eV (Fe 2p3/2) correspond to Fe2+. The other two prominent peaks at 712.9 eV (Fe 2p1/2) and 725.8 eV (Fe 2p3/2) are attributed to Fe3+. These same Fe2+ and Fe3+ peaks are also observed in the N‑GQD/Fe3O4@CoxSy, confirming that Fe3O4 was synthesized before calcination. Besides, for the S 2p spectra of N‑GQD/Fe3O4@CoxSy, the two peaks at 162.15 eV and 163.5 eV of S 2p spectra are ascribed to S2− in Co-S bonds, and the sharp peak at 168.50 eV originates from SO4 2− [61,62]. Notably, the peak area corresponding to SO4 2− in N‑GQD/Fe3O4@Co9S8 is significantly reduced compared to N‑GQD/Fe3O4@CoxSy, suggesting that more SO4 2− was converted to S2− during calcination. This indicates that more Co9S8 was synthesized, consistent with the XRD results. Finally, for the Co 2p spectra, the peaks of the N‑GQD/Fe3O4@Co9S8 at 793.4 eV (Co 2p1/2) and 778.5 eV (Co 2p3/2) correspond to Co3+, and the other two peaks at 781.4 eV (Co 2p1/2) and 797.2 eV (Co 2p3/2) are related to Co2+ [61]. In contrast, for the precursor N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@CoxSy, only two main peaks at 781.2 eV (Co 2p1/2) and 797.2 eV (Co 2p3/2) are observed, which are attributed to Co2+. This indicates that the mixed Co2+/Co3+ modification in N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 enhances electron transfer, resulting in the excellent peroxidase-like activity of the material [43].

The magnetic characteristics of the materials were evaluated using the nanomaterials' hysteresis curves, as depicted in Fig. S4. A vibrating sample magnetometer was used to measure the ferromagnetic properties of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, with a saturation magnetization of 51.9 emu/g. The inset shows how readily the nanomaterials may be separated by applying a magnetic field. It is beneficial to recycle materials after colorimetry to minimize secondary pollution of the environment.

For verifying the catalytic activities of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 as a mimetic oxidase, the catalytic peroxidase performance of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 towards the chromogenic substrates (TMB) was determined in the presence and absence of H2O2. A prominent peroxidase reaction was observed, resulting in a blue color change with a maximum absorption peak around 652 nm when TMB, H2O2, and N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 NPs were mixed for incubation as shown in Fig. 4 A. In the absence of H2O2, the solution showed a light blue color with a weak absorption peak at 652 nm, indicating weak oxidase activity of the material. Under all other conditions, no significant color change was observed.

In order to evaluate their relative catalytic efficiencies, the peroxidase activities of N-GQDs, Fe3O4, N-GQDs/Fe3O4, Co9S8, N-GQDs/Co9S8, and N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 were compared. The results, shown in Fig. S5, indicate that only N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 exhibited excellent peroxidase activity, with its enzyme activity 2–3 times higher than that of Fe3O4 and Co9S8. We speculate that the abundance of active sites in N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 may be the reason for its increased activity, based on the TEM study of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 and Co9S8. The doping of N-GQDs introduces a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which not only enhance the surface activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 but also provide excellent anchoring sites for Co9S8, promoting its uniform growth on the surface of N-GQDs/Fe3O4. This unique structure exposes the active sites of Co9S8, significantly enhancing the catalytic performance of the material. Additionally, the synergistic effect between N-GQDs/Fe3O4 and Co9S8 further amplifies the peroxidase-like activity of the nanocomposites, offering strong support for efficient catalytic reactions.

In order to optimize the enzyme activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, the effects of pH, reaction time, reaction temperature, material volume, and concentrations of H2O2 and TMB on the peroxidase-mimicking activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 were studied. The pH of the system was optimized, and the catalytic activity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 varied with pH, with the best activity at pH 3.5 (Fig. S6A). Second, the absorbance at 652 nm increased gradually with reaction time for the reaction time optimization depicted in Fig. S6B. The rate of increase reduced after 10 min and steadied after 20 min. Therefore, the ideal reaction time of 20 min was determined. From Fig. S6C, it could be observed that temperature had a minimal effect on the system. Although the absorbance reached its maximum value at 35 °C, the difference compared to room temperature was small. For experimental convenience, the room temperature was chosen as the reaction temperature.

For the optimization of the volume of the nanomaterials, the concentration was set at 1 mg/mL, considering the material's solubility. As seen in Fig. S6D, the absorbance at 652 nm of the system increased with the volume of the material, and the rate of increase decreased when the volume of the material reached 50 µL. Given that higher concentrations could affect light transmittance and economic feasibility, 50 µL was selected as the optimal material volume. Finally, we optimized the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB in the system. As shown in Fig. S6E, the absorbance stabilized when the concentration of H2O2 reached 2.5 mM, which was determined to be the optimal concentration, and the optimal concentration of H2O2 was thus determined to be 2.5 mM. Notably, due to the intrinsic bi-enzymatic activity of the material, it could still oxidize TMB to oxTMB even in the absence of H2O2. Therefore, after H2O2 reached saturation, the absorbance continued to increase, but at a slower rate, without fully plateauing. As shown in Fig. S6F, the rate of absorbance increase decreased when the TMB concentration exceeded 125 μM, and the optimal concentration of TMB was thus determined to be 125 μM.

To verify the efficient peroxidase‑like activity of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, the enzyme kinetics evaluation for the reaction was investigated. The apparent steady-state kinetic parameters were determined by varying the concentration of one substrate while keeping the other constant. The Michaelis-Menten curves for the two different substrates, TMB and H2O2, were shown in Fig. 4B and D, respectively, while the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curves were presented in Fig. 4C and E. The maximal response rate (Vmax) and the Mie constant (Km) were determined using the Michaelis-Menten equation. Among these, Km was an important indicator of the affinity between the enzyme and the substrate, with a smaller Km indicating a higher affinity. From Figs. S6A and C, the Km values of the material for H2O2 and TMB were 0.532 mM and 0.163 mM, respectively, and the Vmax values were 3.319 × 10‑6 M/S and 5.297 × 10‑6 M/S, respectively.

In order to comparatively study, the calculated kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax) of the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 and the reported peroxidases were listed in Table 1 . The Km of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 with H2O2 and TMB as substrates was much lower than that of HPR, N-CDs/Fe3O4, Co9S8/C and H2TCPP-Co9S8, suggesting that N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 had a higher affinity toward H2O2 and TMB. Considering the effect of catalyst dosage, K w was adopted as the kinetic parameter for comparison[63], where “w” represents the mass concentration of the catalyst, and K w = Vmax/w. The results show that the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 composite exhibits the highest K w value (Table 1), indicating its superior catalytic performance.

Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and trapping studies were used to examine the potential free radicals in the material to study the catalytic mechanism of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8. First, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine‑N‑oxide (DMPO) was used as the free radical trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 5 A, four characteristic peaks with a 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio, indicate the presence of hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In Fig. 5B, four characteristic peaks with a 1:1:1:1 intensity ratio were observed, corresponding to the presence of superoxide radicals (·O2 −). Additionally, singlet oxygen (1O2) was confirmed using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMP), as shown in Fig. 5C. The detected characteristic peaks exhibited a 1:1:1 ratio, corresponding to the presence of 1O2. After EPR detection, we concluded that ·OH, ·O2 −, and 1O2 were involved in the reaction during the peroxidase activity experiment of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8. To precisely identify the primary free radicals in the catalytic process and reveal their generation mechanism, we also performed a series of free radical trapping experiments.

Different scavengers such as tertiary butanol (·OH scavenger)[4], p-benzoquinone (·O2 − scavenger)[1], and tryptophan (1O2 scavenger)[69] were added to the reaction medium, respectively. In the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 + H2O2 + TMB system, the results show that the presence of ·OH and 1O2 had a small effect, but the p-benzoquinone (·O2 − scavenger), whose amount added was only 40 % of that of the ·OH and 1O2 scavengers, exhibited a visible effect (Figs. S7–S10). This suggests that ·O2 − plays a major role in the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 + H2O2 + TMB system. To determine whether ·OH, ·O2 −and 1O2 are derived from H2O2 or O2, scavengers were added to the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 + TMB system, to investigate the material’s oxidase activity. Only when tryptophan and p-benzoquinone were introduced to the system did the absorbance drop, as seen in Fig. S11.

As a result, as Fig. 5D illustrates, the materials demonstrated an effective peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism. In this mechanism, H2O2 adsorbs onto the surface of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8, where it is activated to generate ·O2 − and ·OH (Eqs. (1) ∼ 2). Under acidic conditions, ·O2 − react with H2O2 to produce ·OH (Eq.3)[48,70,71]. As a result, the activation-generated ·O2 − is partially transformed into ·OH, but it oxidizes TMB more directly. The generation of 1O2 primarily occurs through the excitation of O2 adsorbed on the surface of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 (Eq (4). The O2 adsorbed on the surface of the material is excited to generate 1O2, while some ·O2 − is also produced (Eq (5). Both species work together to oxidize TMB, resulting in the material exhibiting strong peroxidase activity.

After validation, we found that the prepared core–shell nanomaterial N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 exhibited efficient peroxidase-like activity. Based on this, a colorimetric detection system for AA was established, as shown in Scheme 2 ·H2O2 and dissolved oxygen in water are activated on the surface of the material, generating various free radicals. TMB is oxidized by these free radicals to form oxTMB, causing the solution to change from colorless to blue. When AA is added, it reacts with oxTMB, oxidizing AA and losing two hydrogen atoms to form dehydroascorbic acid (DHA). Simultaneously, oxTMB is reduced back to TMB, and the solution changes from blue to colorless. This phenomenon provides a basis for AA detection.

The detection of AA was carried out under optimal detection conditions. As the solubility of AA in the solution increased, the color change and absorption spectra of the system were recorded (Fig. 5E). From Fig. 5F, the solubility of AA in the solution increased from 1 µM to 70 µM, it was evident that the addition of AA exhibited a clear linear relationship with the absorbance at 652 nm. The detection limit (LOD, 3σ/S) of the system was calculated to be 0.404 µM and a good linear correlation was observed within the detection range of 1∼70 µM (R2 = 0.994). In comparison with the colorimetric methods reported in Table S1, our method demonstrated notably high sensitivity and wide detection range.

In order to test the immunity of the colorimetric method to interference, substances that might coexist in the environment were selected for the study, including citric acid (CA), L-arginine, L-histidine, NaCl, glucose, sucrose, KCl, sodium citrate, glycine, L-aspartic acid (ASP), and terephthalic acid (PTA), which were all tested. The results shown in Fig. S12 indicated that only the addition of AA led to a significant change in absorbance, while the interferents, even at concentrations more than 10 times higher than AA, did not affect the system’s absorbance. Therefore, it can be concluded that the proposed system exhibits high selectivity for AA detection.

Given the material's strong magnetic properties, we also assessed its cyclic reproducibility. The peroxidase-like activity of the material was determined again after the material was recovered and washed three times with deionized water and ethanol using a magnet. As shown in Fig. S13, 80 % of the activity was maintained after three cycles, but the activity decreased further after the third repetition. This may have been due to the loss of nanomaterials during the recycling wash. To assess the stability of the material, we tested the peroxidase-like activity at five-day intervals over 35 consecutive days, and the results are shown in Fig. S14. The material maintained more than 90 % of its activity over the 35 days, indicating the long-term stability of N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8.
The results indicate that the prepared N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 nanoparticles exhibit a low detection limit, strong selectivity, good cyclic reproducibility, and long-term stability for AA detection.

To evaluate the feasibility of the system for AA detection, the method was applied to sample solutions from pre-treatment of purchased vitamin C tablets and Water-soluble C100 drink. The primary components of the vitamin C tablets were vitamin C and starch, while the main antioxidant in the Water-soluble C100 drink was vitamin C, with a minor amount of fat-soluble vitamin E. In subsequent experiments, the test substance was diluted multiple times with water to adjust its concentration within the detectable range, effectively minimizing interference from other antioxidants, including vitamin E.

The results are shown in Table 2 below. The calculated ascorbic acid content in the original vitamin C tablet and Water-soluble C100 drink samples was 98.9 mg/tablet and 97.14 mg/(445 mL), respectively, which closely aligned with the values of 100 mg/tablet and 100 mg/(445 mL) indicated in the instruction manuals or ingredient lists. To further verify the accuracy of the system, the AA recoveries from vitamin C tablets and Water-soluble C100 drink were determined using the spiking method. The results, shown in Table 2, indicated that the spiked recoveries ranged from 96.01 % to 103.97 %, demonstrating the system's accuracy in AA detection.

In conclusion, we prepared N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 by hydrothermal method, in which N-GQDs doped Fe3O4 served as the core and Co9S8 was uniformly dispersed in the outer layer of N-GQDs/Fe3O4. The overall material morphology was spherical. The presence of N-GQDs serves two main purposes. On the one hand, the excellent charge transfer ability of N-GQDs can accelerate the charge transfer of the material. On the other hand, a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups are present in N-GQDs. This part of the functional groups plays a role in modulating the morphology during the generation of N-GQDs/Fe3O4. Subsequently, the functional groups in the outer layer of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 provide anchoring sites for Co9S8. The growth of Co9S8, attached to the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 nuclei, is promoted, while the site-blocking effect slows down the convergence of Co9S8 in the outer layer of N-GQDs/Fe3O4, leading to the formation of smaller-sized Co9S8 particles and exposing a large number of active sites. Moreover, Fe3O4 not only provides a magnetic core to facilitate material recovery but, more importantly, the synergistic effect between Fe3O4 and Co9S8 greatly enhances the peroxidase-like activity of the material.

Thus N-GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 exposes active sites that are excited to generate ·O2 – in the presence of H2O2 or O2, which is the primary reason for the material's efficient peroxidase-like activity. Additionally, the material's stabilization kinetics were studied, revealing a stronger substrate affinity compared to horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Based on these findings, we developed a simple AA colorimetric sensor utilizing the material’s peroxidase-like activity. The sensor demonstrated excellent anti-interference ability and a low detection limit of 0.404 µM. It was successfully applied to the detection of vitamin C in tablets and Water-soluble C100 drinks. Moreover, leveraging the magnetic properties of the material, we demonstrated its recyclability, maintaining high activity over three cycles, which significantly reduces material waste and prevents secondary environmental pollution. Furthermore, the material exhibited long-term stability, retaining more than 90 % of its peroxidase-like activity after 35 days. These results indicate that the N‑GQDs/Fe3O4@Co9S8 core–shell nanomaterials we have prepared possess significant potential for applications in environmental monitoring and food quality detection.
